articles.title: Future Mars Rovers Could Mimic a Swimming Motion to Traverse the Planet's Surface
articles.description: Some animals can move efficiently beneath granular surfaces. These include the sandfish (Scincus scincus), a lizard native to the Sahara. It can burrow into the sand and then literally "swim" through the desert sand to hunt or escape predators. German researchers are working on a rover wheel design that mimics that swimming motion. In testing, the wheel system outperformed regular wheels.

articles.title: NASA and ESA Track Record-Breaking 19-Day Solar Radio Burst
articles.description: Learn how NASA spacecraft tracked a record-breaking solar radio burst that circled the Sun for 19 days and what the signal reveals about how solar activity lingers in space.

articles.title: 80-year-old geometry mystery cracked by OpenAI using deep number theory
articles.description: OpenAI has revealed a breakthrough proof that overturns a nearly 80-year-old geometry conjecture posed by Paul Erdős.

articles.title: Hellish Venus-Like Planets May Be More Prevalent Than True ExoEarths
articles.description: Exoplanet hunters are keen to find the next extrasolar earthlike planet, one that may harbor life as we know it. But preliminary results from a new study indicate that our galaxy may be filled with a plethora of exo-Venuses. Yet as one exoplanetary researcher notes: the template for such exo-worlds --- our own Venus --- has been ‘criminally underexplored.’

articles.title: Los Angeles-area wildfires left lead in soil, but how much and where remains contentious
articles.description: The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency said residents should feel assured that most properties cleared by the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers don’t have hazardous amounts of lead. At least one outside scientist is skeptical.

articles.title: Disability inclusion in advertising can build stronger brand affinity
articles.description: Adverts that feature people with a disability greatly enhance consumer attitudes towards brands and their products, according to new research co-authored by Bayes Business School that also shows the effectiveness of diversity regulations and the benefits of compliance. The paper, "Beyond Visibility: The Disability Inclusion Effect in Advertising" is published in the Journal of Marketing.

articles.title: Ocean Census Uncovers 1,121 New Marine Species From Sea Caves to Deep Trenches
articles.description: Learn how the Ocean Census discovered 1,121 new marine species, from deep-sea “ghost sharks” to carnivorous sponges, while accelerating the race to document ocean life.

articles.title: Optoelectronic synapse shows exceptional photoresponse for neuromorphic vision
articles.description: Like so much else in nature, the human visual system has both a complex structure and functional efficiency that is difficult for scientists to replicate. The system is both a sensor and a processor, with the eyes and the brain working together to resolve images with less energy use than anything people have invented.

articles.title: An Ebola outbreak is spreading fast. Should you be worried?
articles.description: A deadly Ebola outbreak in parts of Africa is raising international alarm. Still, experts stress that the chance of a pandemic is low

articles.title: Yellowstone Wolf Pup Numbers Fall to a New Low Since Reintroduction, with a Possible Link to Canine Distemper
articles.description: Learn more about the canine distemper outbreak that could have reduced Yellowstone wolf pup populations to a historic low.

articles.title: Q&A: The path to a PhD in computational science and engineering at MIT
articles.description: MIT doctoral candidate Emily Williams reflects on her time at the Center for Computational Science and Engineering as she becomes the program’s first graduate.

articles.title: SpaceX stacks Starship V3 rocket, completes major fueling test ahead of debut launch
articles.description: SpaceX has stacked its Starship spacecraft and Super Heavy booster at the company's new Starbase launch pad, ahead of the rocket's 12th test flight.

articles.title: Smaller prey, not bigger brains, drove prehistoric tech revolution, Israeli researchers say
articles.description: New study by Tel Aviv University scientists posits disappearance of megaherbivors in the Levant drove humans to develop smaller, more sophisticated tools 200,000 years ago The post Smaller prey, not bigger brains, drove prehistoric tech revolution, Israeli researchers say appeared first on The Times of Israel .

articles.title: New Data Suggests That AI Really Is Already Replacing Human Jobs
articles.description: Ominous. The post New Data Suggests That AI Really Is Already Replacing Human Jobs appeared first on Futurism .

articles.title: US military test-launches nuclear-capable ICBM from California (photos)
articles.description: The U.S. military test-launched an unarmed intercontinental ballistic missile from Vandenberg Space Force Base on May 20, 2026.

articles.title: Pleasanton Students Defeat 100+ Teams In National Mission To Mars Contest
articles.description: Foothill students won Lockheed Martin's nationwide challenge with a scalable nuclear energy concept for Mars settlement.

articles.title: How you map numbers in your mind isn't universal, even among people who read in the same language
articles.description: Imagine taking out a 12-inch ruler and finding that the number 12 is on the left side and the number 1 is on the right side. For most native English speakers, this would be disorienting. We are used to seeing the numbers move from smallest to largest, from left to right. When this layout flips, people struggle because the numbers are now in the "wrong" place.

articles.title: This Moon of Neptune Might Have Survived a Wrecking-Ball Event
articles.description: Neptune’s moons have long provided evidence of a cataclysm four billion years ago. A new study suggests one moon may have made it through.

articles.title: It Looks Like Europa Doesn't Have Plumes of Water Vapour After All
articles.description: In 2014, researchers presented the discovery of water vapour plumes being emitted from Jupiter's moon Europa. This caused quite a stir; it meant that the moon's buried ocean was accessible without contending with the thick ice shell that concealed it. But new research by the same researchers questions those detections.

articles.title: Is It Bad to Use a Laptop Right on Your Lap?
articles.description: Research has dispelled many concerns about laptops, while other risks appear to be more credible.

articles.title: World’s most powerful neutron source unlocks crystals 100x smaller with 2.8 MW boost
articles.description: The US' Spallation Neutron Source (SNS) has reached 2.8 megawatts (MW), allowing scientists to run faster experiments and study tiny samples.

articles.title: Scientists bring crop protection research to local science classes
articles.description: As the world’s population continues to grow, farmers across the globe are working hard to feed more than 8 billion humans. Agriculture at that scale requires interventions, including herbicides and pesticides to keep crops healthy and free from disease and insect infestations. Chemical herbicides and pesticides were, and are, a common solution to these problems, but people are becoming more concerned about the environmental and health impacts of these products.

articles.title: NASA satellite to test space ‘gas station’ tech for Moon and Mars trips
articles.description: NASA's LOXSAT will test the technologies required to refuel in Earth's orbit, enabling large crewed missions to the moon and Mars.

articles.title: ‘Perfect Storm’: How Trump's Aid Cuts Are Fueling the Ebola Outbreak
articles.description: One health provider who works on the ground says that basic medical equipment like masks and hand sanitizers are in short supply due to funding cuts.

articles.title: Tru-1 By BlueGenes Launches to Advance Precision Health Through Smarter Genetic Insights
articles.description: SCOTTSDALE, Ariz., May 20, 2026 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Tru-1 by BlueGenes today announced its official launch, introducing a new precision health platform designed to help healthcare providers, employers, and patients better understand how genetic insights can support personalized treatment and medication decisions. The launch of Tru-1 comes at a time when healthcare organizations are increasingly seeking data-driven approaches to improve patient outcomes, reduce avoidable adverse drug reactions, and support more personalized care strategies. Tru-1 combines advanced genetic testing capabilities with actionable health insights to help organizations and individuals make more informed healthcare decisions. The company's platform is focused on improving visibility into how genetics may influence medication effectiveness, wellness outcomes, and long-term care planning. "Healthcare is moving toward a more personalized future, and patients deserve solutions built ... Full story available on Benzinga.com

articles.description: Is there any significance to the fat percentage in the milk you drink? Has the myth that milk causes cancer been scientifically proven, and at what age should you reconsider your milk consumption?

articles.title: Britain’s 11,000-year-old “oldest northerner” was a 3-year-old girl, DNA reveals
articles.description: Scientists have identified the oldest known human remains in Northern Britain as a young girl who lived around 11,000 years ago. Found in a Cumbrian cave and nicknamed the “Ossick Lass,” she was likely between 2.5 and 3.5 years old when she died. Nearby jewelry and evidence of multiple burials suggest the cave held deep spiritual importance for some of Britain’s earliest hunter-gatherers. The discovery is shedding new light on life — and death — just after the Ice Age.
